content.text: Status Page Update Cadence During Incidents

The status page is the primary channel through which customers learn about service disruptions. Its credibility depends entirely on consistent, timely, and accurate updates. A status page that goes silent for two hours during an active P0 is worse than no status page at all: it tells customers the company has lost control of the situation or has stopped caring about communication.

Cadence -- the rhythm of updates -- matters as much as content. Customers who know they will receive an update every 30 minutes can plan around the disruption. Customers who receive irregular, unpredictable updates cannot. This document defines the mandatory cadence rules for each status state and severity combination, and the process for maintaining them mechanically without burdening the IC.

attrs.alt: Status page lifecycle: four states (Investigating, Identified, Monitoring, Resolved) arranged on a timeline with update interval tick marks. Investigating requires updates every 30 min (15 min for P0); Identified on change or 30 min; Monitoring and Resolved on transition only.

content.text: Every status page incident passes through four states in order. Skipping a state is only permitted when the transition conditions for both the skipped and the target state are simultaneously met, which must be documented explicitly in the update.
The incident has been declared and acknowledged publicly. Root cause is unknown. The team is actively working to understand what is happening.
Within 60 minutes of a P0/P1 declaration. Within 2 hours for a customer-visible P2. Immediately for any incident where customers are already reporting issues publicly.
Every 30 minutes for P1/P2. Every 15 minutes for P0. Each update must contain: current observed impact, investigation direction, and next update time.
Root cause has been confirmed by the technical team. The team is implementing a fix or mitigation. Resolution ETA may or may not be available.
As soon as the IC confirms root cause with the Tech Lead. Do not wait until the fix is deployed.
On transition (when entering Identified) and then every 30 minutes, or sooner if a material change occurs (fix deployed, ETA revised, scope changes).
Mitigation or fix has been deployed. The team is observing metrics to confirm full resolution before declaring the incident closed.
When the IC declares mitigation in place. All de-escalation observation windows start from this point.
One update on transition. No further updates unless the incident deteriorates and must move back to Identified.
All de-escalation gates have passed. The incident is closed. The status page shows the full impact window and a brief description of what happened.
When the IC runs /ic close and all gates confirm passed.
One final update on resolution. No further updates unless the incident reopens.

The SLA on first update is measured from declaration, not from when the status page team is notified. The IC bot starts this clock automatically at declaration and sends the Comms Lead a reminder at the halfway point (15 min for P0, 30 min for P1).

Every status page update must contain specific elements to be approved by the IC. Updates that are missing required elements must be revised before publication. The Comms Lead runs through this checklist before submitting each update for IC approval.
What customers are experiencing. No technical jargon. 'Some API requests are returning errors' not 'the inference gateway is dropping connections at the load balancer layer'.
That the team is actively investigating. The word 'investigating' must appear in the update.
Which specific services or endpoints are affected. Do not say 'all services' unless confirmed.
Specific timestamp, not 'shortly' or 'as soon as possible'. 'We will provide the next update by 15:30 UTC.'
One to two sentences on what went wrong, non-technical. Do not name internal systems.
'A fix is being implemented' or 'A fix has been deployed and we are monitoring the results'.
Include if the Tech Lead can provide a reliable estimate. Omit if unknown rather than guessing.
Whether customers are still experiencing issues at the time of this update.
Start time and end time of the incident in UTC.
Brief summary of affected services and customer impact.
Sincere apology for the disruption. Required for P0 and P1.
'We will publish a full postmortem within 5 business days.'
Manually tracking update cadence during an active incident is error-prone. The IC bot manages cadence enforcement through timed reminders and a cadence dashboard visible in the bridge channel.
The IC bot posts a pinned cadence dashboard message in the bridge channel that updates in real time:

## Status Page Cadence -- INC-20240719-004

Current state: Investigating (P1)
Last update:   14:12 UTC (18 min ago)
Next due:      14:42 UTC (in 12 min)
Overdue:       No

Updates posted: 2
-- 13:10 UTC  Investigating (initial)
-- 13:42 UTC  Investigating (30-min update)
-- 14:12 UTC  Investigating (30-min update)   <-- latest

Comms Lead: @alice
IC approval gate: required before publish

A missed update -- one posted more than 5 minutes after the scheduled time -- is recorded in the incident record. Three or more cadence misses in a single incident trigger a postmortem action item for the Comms Lead and are reviewed in the monthly reliability retrospective.

When a cadence miss is detected, the Comms Lead posts the overdue update immediately with a note acknowledging the delay. Do not skip the overdue update and wait for the next scheduled slot -- that doubles the gap.

We apologise for the delayed update. Our team continues to investigate the elevated error rates affecting the Claude API. We expect to identify the root cause within the next 30 minutes and will provide a further update by 16:00 UTC.

During a volatile P0 -- metrics swinging, root cause changing, high executive scrutiny -- the Comms Lead may feel that posting an update before they have 'something new to say' is pointless. This is the single most common reason for cadence misses and it is incorrect reasoning. An update that says 'investigation is ongoing; no new information at this time' is more valuable to customers than silence: it confirms the team is still engaged and has not lost the thread.
The minimum acceptable update during the Investigating state when there is nothing new to report:
Investigation continues. Our engineering team is actively working to identify the root cause. We will provide the next update by {timestamp}.

When the IC updates the severity or state internally (bridge channel, exec channel), the status page must be updated within 10 minutes to match. A mismatch between internal severity and external status page erodes trust when customers compare internal communications with public ones.
Post new Investigating update noting expanded impact
Transition to Resolved state with impact window
Reopen status page incident; move back to Investigating

Customers can subscribe to status page notifications via email or RSS. Subscriber management directly affects how many customers receive proactive notification versus discover the incident by experiencing it. A higher subscriber rate reduces inbound support ticket volume during incidents.
Customers subscribe per component (Claude API, Console, Anthropic.com). An email is sent automatically when any subscribed component transitions state.
Machine-readable feed at status.anthropic.com/history.rss. Enterprise customers with monitoring integrations typically use this.
GET /api/v2/status.json returns current status in structured JSON. Some enterprise customers poll this endpoint from their own monitoring infrastructure.
The status page provider supports webhook delivery to customer Slack workspaces. Available for enterprise customers who configure it in their account settings.
Status Page API for Monitoring Integration

import requests
from dataclasses import dataclass

STATUS_PAGE_URL = "https://status.anthropic.com/api/v2/status.json"
COMPONENTS_URL = "https://status.anthropic.com/api/v2/components.json"

@dataclass
class ComponentStatus:
    id: str
    name: str
    status: str           # operational | degraded_performance | partial_outage | major_outage
    updated_at: str

def get_claude_api_status() -> ComponentStatus:
    """Fetch current status for the Claude API component."""
    response = requests.get(COMPONENTS_URL, timeout=5)
    response.raise_for_status()
    components = response.json()["components"]

    claude_api = next(
        (c for c in components if c["name"] == "Claude API"),
        None,
    )
    if claude_api is None:
        raise ValueError("Claude API component not found in status page")

    return ComponentStatus(
        id=claude_api["id"],
        name=claude_api["name"],
        status=claude_api["status"],
        updated_at=claude_api["updated_at"],
    )

def is_degraded() -> bool:
    """Returns True if the Claude API is not fully operational."""
    status = get_claude_api_status()
    return status.status != "operational"
Writing Quality: Common Status Page Failures
Poor status page writing reduces customer trust even when the underlying incident is being resolved competently. The following failures recur across incidents and have specific fixes.
The inference gateway is experiencing elevated 5xx rates due to OOM in the serving fleet
Most customers do not know what these terms mean; creates anxiety without information
Some API requests are failing. Our engineering team has identified the affected service and is working on a fix.
'Shortly' means nothing; customers cannot plan around it
We will provide the next update by 15:30 UTC.
All Claude API requests are affected
If only 30% of requests are affected, overclaiming causes unnecessary alarm
A portion of Claude API requests are experiencing errors. We are investigating the scope.
Identified -- we believe the cause is X
'Believe' signals uncertainty; Identified state should only be used when the cause is confirmed
Stay in Investigating until the Tech Lead has confirmed the root cause.
A terse resolution with no acknowledgement of customer impact reads as indifference
This incident has been resolved. We apologise for the disruption and will publish a postmortem within 5 business days.
Status page tone should be factual, direct, and empathetic in equal measure. It should not be:
"Due to an unusual combination of factors..." implies the company is making excuses before explaining what happened.
"Our world-class engineering team is actively engaged..." The incident is not the time for brand voice.
"We are unable to confirm or deny impact to any specific customer at this time." Reads as evasion.
"Critical systems failure affecting all users." Severity should be conveyed through content, not dramatic word choice.

After every P0 or P1 incident, the Comms Lead conducts a cadence audit as part of the postmortem preparation. The audit reviews each update against the requirements and produces a score that feeds the monthly comms metrics review.
Was the update posted within the cadence window? Record the actual elapsed time.
Did the update contain all required content elements for its state?
Did the update contain a specific next-update timestamp?
Did the update avoid jargon (no internal system names, no stack trace references)?
Was the scope of impact accurately described at the time of posting?
Did the Resolved update include the correct impact window (start and end time)?

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class StatusUpdateAuditResult:
    update_index: int
    timestamp: str
    state: str
    on_time: bool
    elapsed_minutes: float
    missing_elements: List[str]
    scope_accurate: bool
    score: float           # 0.0 to 1.0

def score_update(result: StatusUpdateAuditResult) -> float:
    """Score an individual status page update 0.0-1.0."""
    points = 0
    total = 5

    if result.on_time:
        points += 1
    if not result.missing_elements:
        points += 2          # content requirements worth double
    if result.scope_accurate:
        points += 1
    # No jargon check is binary -- handled in missing_elements
    points += max(0, 1 - (len(result.missing_elements) * 0.25))

    return round(points / total, 2)

def audit_incident_updates(updates: List[dict]) -> dict:
    """Return aggregate audit results for a single incident."""
    results = [audit_single_update(u) for u in updates]
    scores = [score_update(r) for r in results]
    return {
        "update_count": len(updates),
        "on_time_rate": sum(1 for r in results if r.on_time) / len(results),
        "mean_score": sum(scores) / len(scores),
        "missed_cadence_count": sum(1 for r in results if not r.on_time),
    }

Status page cadence is a commitment to customers, not a best-effort goal. The four states -- Investigating, Identified, Monitoring, Resolved -- each have defined entry conditions and update cadences: P0 requires updates every 15 minutes during Investigating; P1 every 30 minutes. Each update must satisfy content requirements: impact statement, investigation status, and a specific next-update timestamp. The IC bot tracks cadence automatically, posts reminders when updates are due, and records misses in the incident record. Cross-channel consistency requires that status page state transitions happen within 10 minutes of corresponding internal decisions. A minimum acceptable update during a dry period ('investigation ongoing; next update at X') is always better than silence.
